questions.title: Purpose of a load balancer
questions.description: What is the primary purpose of placing a load balancer in front of a pool of identical application servers?
questions.explanation: A load balancer distributes incoming requests across multiple backend servers so no single instance is overwhelmed, improving throughput and availability.

options.text: To distribute incoming traffic across multiple servers
To permanently store user session data
To compile application code before deployment
To encrypt the database at rest

questions.title: Horizontal vs vertical scaling
questions.description: Which statement best describes horizontal scaling?
questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling (scaling out) adds more machines to the pool, whereas vertical scaling (scaling up) adds more resources to a single machine.

options.text: Adding more machines to share the load
Adding more CPU and RAM to a single machine
Rewriting the app in a faster language
Reducing the number of running services

questions.title: What a cache is for
questions.description: Why would you add a cache layer such as Redis in front of a database?
questions.explanation: Caches store frequently accessed data in fast memory to reduce latency and offload repeated reads from the database.

options.text: To serve frequently requested data faster and reduce database load
To guarantee strong consistency for all writes
To replace the need for a primary database entirely
To increase write durability on disk

questions.title: Stateless services and scaling
questions.description: Why are stateless application servers easier to scale horizontally?
questions.explanation: Because they hold no client-specific state between requests, any instance can serve any request, so you can add or remove instances freely behind a load balancer.

options.text: Any instance can handle any request, so instances are interchangeable
They never need a database connection
They automatically encrypt all traffic
They use less disk because they store all data in RAM

questions.title: CAP theorem core trade-off
questions.description: According to the CAP theorem, when a network partition occurs, a distributed system must choose between which two properties?
questions.explanation: During a partition you cannot have both consistency and availability; you must sacrifice one. CAP says: in the presence of a Partition, choose Consistency or Availability.

options.text: Consistency and availability
Latency and throughput
Durability and partition tolerance
Security and performance

questions.title: Read replicas and scaling reads
questions.description: How do read replicas help a read-heavy application scale?
questions.explanation: Read replicas copy data from the primary and serve read queries, distributing read load while the primary handles writes.

options.text: Read queries are spread across replicas, offloading the primary
They allow concurrent writes to every replica
They eliminate the need for caching
They guarantee zero replication lag

questions.title: Latency vs throughput
questions.description: Which pair of definitions correctly distinguishes latency from throughput?
questions.explanation: Latency is the time to complete a single operation; throughput is the number of operations completed per unit of time.

options.text: Latency is time per request; throughput is requests per unit time
Latency is requests per second; throughput is time per request
Both measure the same thing in different units
Latency measures disk space; throughput measures CPU usage

questions.title: Cache eviction with LRU
questions.description: What does a Least Recently Used (LRU) cache eviction policy do when the cache is full?
questions.explanation: LRU evicts the entry that has not been accessed for the longest time, assuming recently used items are more likely to be used again.

options.text: Evicts the entry that was accessed least recently
Evicts the largest entry by size
Evicts a random entry
Evicts the most recently added entry

questions.title: Consistent hashing benefit
questions.description: What problem does consistent hashing primarily solve in a distributed cache or storage cluster?
questions.explanation: Consistent hashing minimizes the number of keys that must be remapped when nodes are added or removed, avoiding a full reshuffle of the keyspace.

options.text: It minimizes key remapping when nodes join or leave
It guarantees perfectly even load with no virtual nodes
It removes the need for replication
It encrypts keys before hashing

questions.title: Eventual consistency meaning
questions.description: In an eventually consistent system, what guarantee is provided?
questions.explanation: Eventual consistency guarantees that, absent new updates, all replicas will converge to the same value over time, but reads may be stale in the interim.

options.text: Replicas converge to the same value given enough time without new writes
Every read always returns the most recent write immediately
Writes are rejected until all replicas agree
Data is never replicated to other nodes

questions.title: Cache-aside pattern
questions.description: In the cache-aside (lazy loading) pattern, what happens on a cache miss?
questions.explanation: On a miss the application reads from the database, then populates the cache so subsequent reads hit the cache.

options.text: The app loads from the database and then writes the value into the cache
The cache automatically queries the database itself
The request fails until the cache is warmed manually
The write is sent only to the cache, never the database

questions.title: Thundering herd / cache stampede
questions.description: A popular cache key expires and thousands of concurrent requests all miss and hit the database at once. What is this problem called?
questions.explanation: This is a cache stampede (thundering herd). Mitigations include request coalescing, locks, or staggered/early recomputation of TTLs.

options.text: A cache stampede (thundering herd)
A split-brain
A cold start
A head-of-line block

questions.title: Write-through vs write-back cache
questions.description: What is the key risk of a write-back (write-behind) cache compared to write-through?
questions.explanation: Write-back acknowledges the write after updating only the cache and flushes to the store later, so a crash before the flush can lose data.

options.text: Data buffered in the cache can be lost if the node crashes before flushing
It always has higher write latency than write-through
It cannot be used with an LRU policy
It prevents the cache from ever being read

questions.title: Availability and the 'nines'
questions.description: Roughly how much downtime per year does 99.9% ('three nines') availability allow?
questions.explanation: Three nines permits about 8.76 hours of downtime per year (0.1% of 8760 hours).

options.text: About 8.8 hours
About 5 minutes
About 3.65 days
About 88 hours

questions.title: Reverse proxy role
questions.description: Which function is commonly handled by a reverse proxy?
questions.explanation: A reverse proxy sits in front of servers and can terminate TLS, route requests, cache responses, and balance load.

options.text: TLS termination and request routing to backend servers
Storing the canonical copy of all user data
Running database migrations automatically
Generating application source code

questions.title: Idempotency definition
questions.description: What does it mean for an operation to be idempotent?
questions.explanation: An idempotent operation produces the same result whether performed once or multiple times, which is important for safe retries.

options.text: Performing it multiple times has the same effect as performing it once
It can only ever be executed a single time
It always returns a different result each call
It must complete within a fixed time budget

questions.title: Back-of-the-envelope estimation
questions.description: Why do system design interviews emphasize back-of-the-envelope capacity estimation?
questions.explanation: Rough estimates of QPS, storage, and bandwidth justify architectural choices like sharding, caching, or CDN use before committing to a design.

options.text: To size the system and justify components like caches, shards, and CDNs
To produce exact production capacity numbers
To replace load testing entirely
To decide the programming language

questions.title: Sticky sessions trade-off
questions.description: What is a drawback of using sticky sessions (session affinity) on a load balancer?
questions.explanation: Pinning a user to one server uneven-loads the pool and loses the user's session if that server fails, undermining horizontal scaling benefits.

options.text: Load can become uneven and a server failure drops that user's session
It makes all servers fully stateless
It guarantees perfectly even distribution
It removes the need for a load balancer

questions.title: Strong consistency cost
questions.description: What is a typical cost of providing strong consistency across geographically distributed replicas?
questions.explanation: Coordinating replicas before acknowledging a write (e.g., quorum or consensus across regions) increases write latency and can reduce availability during partitions.

options.text: Higher write latency and reduced availability during partitions
Unlimited write throughput
Zero replication overhead
Automatic conflict-free merging at no cost

questions.title: CDN primary benefit
questions.description: What is the primary benefit of serving static assets through a CDN?
questions.explanation: CDNs cache content at edge locations close to users, reducing latency and offloading origin servers.

options.text: Lower latency by serving content from edge locations near users
Stronger database consistency
Automatic schema migrations
Guaranteed real-time write propagation

questions.title: Graceful degradation
questions.description: What does graceful degradation mean in system design?
questions.explanation: When a dependency fails, the system continues to provide reduced functionality rather than failing completely (e.g., serving stale cache data).

options.text: Continuing to provide reduced functionality when a component fails
Shutting down all services to protect data
Automatically scaling up to infinite capacity
Rejecting all requests during partial failures

questions.title: Health checks in load balancing
questions.description: Why does a load balancer perform periodic health checks on backend instances?
questions.explanation: Health checks let the load balancer stop routing traffic to unhealthy instances, improving availability.

options.text: To stop sending traffic to unhealthy instances
To back up the database
To compile new code on each instance
To rotate TLS certificates

questions.title: Quorum reads and writes
questions.description: In a Dynamo-style system with N replicas, what does the condition W + R > N guarantee?
questions.explanation: When the write quorum plus read quorum exceeds the replica count, the read set and write set overlap on at least one node, so reads can observe the latest write.

options.text: Read and write quorums overlap, so reads can see the latest write
All replicas are always perfectly synchronized
Writes never need to contact more than one node
The system can tolerate any number of failures

questions.title: SQL vs NoSQL choice
questions.description: Which scenario most favors a relational (SQL) database over a typical NoSQL store?
questions.explanation: Workloads with complex relationships, multi-row transactions, and ad-hoc joins fit relational databases well.

options.text: Highly relational data needing multi-row ACID transactions and joins
Massive append-only logs with a simple key lookup
Schema-less documents that change shape frequently
Purely in-memory ephemeral counters

questions.title: ACID atomicity
questions.description: What does the 'A' (Atomicity) in ACID guarantee for a transaction?
questions.explanation: Atomicity ensures all operations in a transaction either complete fully or have no effect at all; partial application is impossible.

options.text: All operations commit together or none do
Transactions execute as fast as possible
Data is stored on at least two disks
Every read returns committed data only

questions.title: Database index purpose
questions.description: What is the primary purpose of adding an index to a database column?
questions.explanation: An index speeds up lookups and range queries on that column by avoiding full table scans, at the cost of extra storage and slower writes.

options.text: To speed up read queries that filter or sort on that column
To compress the entire table
To make all writes faster
To enforce that the column is encrypted

questions.title: Index write trade-off
questions.description: What is a cost of adding many indexes to a frequently written table?
questions.explanation: Every insert/update/delete must also update each index, so write performance degrades and storage grows as index count increases.

options.text: Writes become slower because each index must be maintained
Reads on indexed columns become slower
The table can no longer be queried
Transactions lose atomicity

questions.title: Sharding definition
questions.description: What is database sharding?
questions.explanation: Sharding horizontally partitions a dataset across multiple databases/nodes, each holding a subset of the rows, to scale beyond one machine.

options.text: Splitting data horizontally across multiple databases by a shard key
Copying the full dataset to every node
Compressing old rows into archive files
Adding indexes to every column

questions.title: Choosing a shard key
questions.description: What is the main risk of choosing a poor shard key?
questions.explanation: A skewed shard key concentrates data and traffic on a few shards (hot shards), defeating the purpose of horizontal partitioning.

options.text: Hot shards: uneven data and traffic distribution
It prevents the use of any index
It automatically violates ACID
It doubles storage on every shard

questions.title: Normalization goal
questions.description: What is the main goal of normalizing a relational schema?
questions.explanation: Normalization reduces data redundancy and update anomalies by organizing data into related tables with foreign keys.

options.text: Reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies
Maximize duplication for faster reads
Eliminate the need for primary keys
Store all data in a single wide table

questions.title: Denormalization for reads
questions.description: Why might you deliberately denormalize data in a read-heavy system?
questions.explanation: Denormalization duplicates data to avoid expensive joins at read time, trading write complexity and storage for faster reads.

options.text: To avoid expensive joins and speed up reads
To guarantee third normal form
To reduce total storage used
To make writes simpler

questions.title: B-tree index strength
questions.description: Which query pattern does a B-tree index handle efficiently?
questions.explanation: B-tree indexes keep keys in sorted order, making them efficient for equality and range queries (e.g., BETWEEN, ORDER BY).

options.text: Range and ordered lookups such as BETWEEN and ORDER BY
Full-text fuzzy matching across documents
Geospatial nearest-neighbor search
Approximate set membership tests

questions.title: Object storage use case
questions.description: Which is the best fit for an object store like Amazon S3?
questions.explanation: Object stores excel at storing large, immutable blobs (images, videos, backups) accessed by key, not transactional row updates.

options.text: Storing large files like images, videos, and backups
Running complex multi-table joins
Enforcing foreign key constraints
Sub-millisecond transactional counters

questions.title: Connection pooling
questions.description: Why do applications use a database connection pool?
questions.explanation: Establishing connections is expensive; a pool reuses a bounded set of open connections, reducing latency and protecting the database from connection exhaustion.

options.text: To reuse connections and limit concurrent load on the database
To encrypt every query automatically
To shard data across nodes
To cache query results in the application

questions.title: Replication lag impact
questions.description: An app writes to the primary then immediately reads from a replica and sees stale data. What is the cause?
questions.explanation: Asynchronous replication means a replica may not yet have applied the latest write, so a read-after-write on a replica can be stale (replication lag).

options.text: Replication lag between primary and replica
The index is corrupted
The transaction was not atomic
The shard key was wrong

questions.title: Write-ahead log (WAL)
questions.description: What is the primary purpose of a write-ahead log in a database?
questions.explanation: The WAL records changes durably before applying them to data files, enabling crash recovery and durability without flushing every page synchronously.

options.text: To ensure durability and enable crash recovery
To speed up read-only queries
To compress historical data
To replace the need for indexes

questions.title: LSM tree write advantage
questions.description: Why are LSM-tree-based stores (e.g., Cassandra, RocksDB) well suited to write-heavy workloads?
questions.explanation: LSM trees buffer writes in memory and flush them sequentially to disk, turning random writes into fast sequential I/O, with compaction merging segments later.

options.text: They convert random writes into fast sequential disk writes
They never need compaction
They store all data in a single sorted file forever
They avoid using memory entirely

questions.title: Isolation level: read committed
questions.description: Under the READ COMMITTED isolation level, which anomaly is still possible?
questions.explanation: READ COMMITTED prevents dirty reads but allows non-repeatable reads, where re-reading a row within a transaction can return different committed values.

options.text: Non-repeatable reads
Dirty reads
Lost updates are impossible by definition
No anomalies are possible

questions.title: Time-series database fit
questions.description: Which workload is a time-series database optimized for?
questions.explanation: Time-series databases optimize for high-volume timestamped writes and time-window aggregations, like metrics and IoT sensor data.

options.text: High-volume timestamped metrics with time-range queries
Complex many-to-many relational joins
Full-text document search
Storing large binary blobs

questions.title: Foreign key constraint role
questions.description: What does a foreign key constraint enforce?
questions.explanation: A foreign key ensures referential integrity: a value in the child table must reference an existing row in the parent table.

options.text: Referential integrity between related tables
That a column value is unique
That a column is indexed
That writes are encrypted

questions.title: Partitioning vs replication
questions.description: What is the key difference between partitioning and replication?
questions.explanation: Partitioning splits data into disjoint subsets across nodes (for scale); replication copies the same data to multiple nodes (for availability/read scaling).

options.text: Partitioning splits data into subsets; replication copies the same data
They are two names for the same thing
Replication splits data; partitioning copies it
Both reduce total storage usage

questions.title: Optimistic concurrency control
questions.description: How does optimistic concurrency control typically detect a conflicting update?
questions.explanation: Optimistic concurrency reads a version/timestamp, then on write checks it is unchanged; if it changed, the update is rejected and the caller retries.

options.text: By checking a version number or timestamp at write time
By locking the row for the entire transaction
By disabling all other connections
By replicating the row before writing

questions.title: Materialized view
questions.description: What is a materialized view?
questions.explanation: A materialized view stores the precomputed result of a query physically, speeding up reads at the cost of needing refresh when underlying data changes.

options.text: A precomputed, stored query result that must be refreshed
A virtual table computed fresh on every query
An in-memory cache managed by the OS
A backup copy of the entire database

questions.title: Hot partition mitigation
questions.description: A key-value store has one partition receiving most traffic. Which technique helps distribute the load?
questions.explanation: Adding a salt/suffix to the key spreads writes for a hot key across multiple partitions, reducing the hot-partition bottleneck.

options.text: Salting the key to spread it across multiple partitions
Removing all replicas
Increasing the isolation level
Disabling the cache

questions.title: Blob in DB vs object store
questions.description: Why is it usually better to store large media in object storage and keep only a URL/reference in the relational database?
questions.explanation: Storing large blobs in a relational DB bloats backups, slows queries, and wastes expensive transactional storage; a reference keeps the DB lean.

options.text: It keeps the database small and lets a CDN/object store serve media efficiently
Object stores enforce foreign keys better
Relational databases cannot store binary data at all
It improves transaction isolation

questions.title: Two-phase commit drawback
questions.description: What is a well-known drawback of two-phase commit (2PC) for distributed transactions?
questions.explanation: 2PC is blocking: if the coordinator fails after prepare, participants may hold locks indefinitely awaiting the decision, hurting availability.

options.text: It can block participants holding locks if the coordinator fails
It cannot guarantee atomicity
It requires no coordinator
It eliminates network communication

questions.title: Composite index column order
questions.description: For a composite index on (a, b), which query can use the index efficiently for filtering?
questions.explanation: A composite index can be used for the leftmost prefix; filtering on `a` (or `a` and `b`) uses it, but filtering on `b` alone generally cannot.

options.text: A query filtering on column a (the leftmost prefix)
A query filtering only on column b
Any query regardless of columns used
Only queries that select all columns

questions.title: Message queue purpose
questions.description: What is the primary benefit of putting a message queue between a producer and a consumer service?
questions.explanation: A queue decouples producer and consumer, smoothing load spikes and letting the consumer process work asynchronously at its own pace.

options.text: It decouples services and buffers load so work is processed asynchronously
It guarantees synchronous responses to the producer
It replaces the need for a database
It encrypts all messages by default

questions.title: At-least-once delivery implication
questions.description: If a message queue provides at-least-once delivery, what must consumers handle?
questions.explanation: At-least-once means a message may be delivered more than once, so consumers must be idempotent to handle duplicates safely.

options.text: Duplicate messages, by being idempotent
Messages arriving strictly once and in order
Messages that are never delivered
Automatic deduplication with no effort

questions.title: Pub/sub vs point-to-point
questions.description: How does publish/subscribe messaging differ from a point-to-point queue?
questions.explanation: In pub/sub, each message is delivered to all subscribers of a topic; in a point-to-point queue, each message is consumed by exactly one consumer.

options.text: Pub/sub fans a message out to all subscribers; a queue gives it to one consumer
Pub/sub guarantees one consumer; queues fan out to all
They behave identically
Pub/sub cannot have more than one subscriber

questions.title: Consensus algorithm role
questions.description: What problem do consensus algorithms like Raft and Paxos solve?
questions.explanation: They let a group of nodes agree on a single value/log order despite failures, enabling consistent replication and leader election.

options.text: Getting multiple nodes to agree on a value despite failures
Compressing network packets
Encrypting data at rest
Indexing a database table

questions.title: Leader election purpose
questions.description: Why do many distributed systems elect a single leader node?
questions.explanation: A single leader serializes writes and coordinates replication, simplifying consistency compared to accepting writes on any node.

options.text: To serialize writes and coordinate replication through one authority
To increase the number of single points of failure
To allow every node to accept writes independently
To remove the need for replication

questions.title: Split-brain problem
questions.description: What is a 'split-brain' scenario in a distributed cluster?
questions.explanation: A network partition causes two subsets of nodes to each believe they are the leader/primary, risking divergent, conflicting writes.

options.text: A partition causes two nodes to both act as leader, risking conflicting writes
A single node runs out of memory
The cache and database disagree on a value
A query plan chooses the wrong index

questions.title: Dead letter queue
questions.description: What is a dead letter queue (DLQ) used for?
questions.explanation: A DLQ captures messages that repeatedly fail processing so they can be inspected and reprocessed without blocking the main queue.

options.text: Holding messages that repeatedly fail so they can be inspected later
Storing successfully processed messages permanently
Encrypting messages in transit
Load balancing across consumers

questions.title: Event sourcing concept
questions.description: What is the core idea behind event sourcing?
questions.explanation: Event sourcing stores state as an immutable, append-only sequence of events; current state is derived by replaying those events.

options.text: Persisting state as an append-only log of events that can be replayed
Overwriting the current state on every change with no history
Storing only the latest snapshot and discarding events
Caching events in memory only

questions.title: CQRS pattern
questions.description: What does CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) separate?
questions.explanation: CQRS separates the write model (commands) from the read model (queries), allowing each to be optimized and scaled independently.

options.text: The write (command) model from the read (query) model
The frontend from the backend
The cache from the database
Authentication from authorization

questions.title: Saga pattern
questions.description: How does the saga pattern manage a transaction that spans multiple microservices?
questions.explanation: A saga breaks the workflow into local transactions, each with a compensating action to undo prior steps if a later step fails, avoiding distributed locks.

options.text: As a series of local transactions with compensating actions on failure
By holding a single global lock across all services
By using two-phase commit across every service
By disabling transactions entirely

questions.title: Heartbeat mechanism
questions.description: What is the role of heartbeats in a distributed system?
questions.explanation: Periodic heartbeat signals let nodes detect that peers are alive; a missed heartbeat triggers failure detection and possibly failover.

options.text: To detect node failures by monitoring periodic liveness signals
To synchronize database schemas
To encrypt inter-node traffic
To compress messages

questions.title: Idempotency key in APIs
questions.description: How does an idempotency key make a payment API safe to retry?
questions.explanation: The server records the key with the first request's result; repeated requests with the same key return the stored result instead of charging again.

options.text: The server dedupes repeated requests with the same key, returning the original result
It encrypts the payment amount
It guarantees the request never fails
It speeds up the database write

questions.title: Exactly-once delivery reality
questions.description: Which statement about 'exactly-once' message delivery is most accurate?
questions.explanation: True end-to-end exactly-once delivery over an unreliable network is generally impossible; it's approximated via at-least-once delivery plus idempotent processing or transactional deduplication.

options.text: It is typically approximated with at-least-once delivery plus idempotent consumers
It is trivially provided by all message brokers for free
It requires no consumer-side deduplication
It is the same as at-most-once delivery

questions.title: Backpressure
questions.description: What does backpressure refer to in a streaming or queue-based system?
questions.explanation: Backpressure is a mechanism by which a slow consumer signals upstream producers to slow down, preventing unbounded buffering and resource exhaustion.

options.text: A slow consumer signaling producers to slow down to avoid overload
Pushing all data as fast as possible regardless of consumers
Encrypting the data stream
Replaying failed messages forever

questions.title: Vector clocks
questions.description: What do vector clocks help determine in a distributed system?
questions.explanation: Vector clocks capture causality between events across nodes, letting the system detect concurrent updates that may conflict.

options.text: The causal ordering of events and whether updates are concurrent
The exact wall-clock time of every event
The amount of free disk on each node
The network latency between nodes

questions.title: Service discovery
questions.description: What problem does service discovery solve in a microservices architecture?
questions.explanation: Service discovery lets services find the current network locations of other services, which change as instances scale, move, or fail.

options.text: Locating healthy instances of a service whose addresses change dynamically
Choosing which database index to use
Compiling service code
Encrypting service-to-service traffic

questions.title: Clock skew problem
questions.description: Why is relying on synchronized wall-clock timestamps across servers risky for ordering events?
questions.explanation: Server clocks drift and are only loosely synchronized (clock skew), so timestamp ordering can be wrong; logical clocks are preferred for causal ordering.

options.text: Clocks drift between machines, so timestamp ordering can be incorrect
Timestamps consume too much storage
Wall clocks are always perfectly synchronized
Timestamps cannot be compared at all

questions.title: Bulkhead pattern
questions.description: What does the bulkhead pattern achieve in a resilient system?
questions.explanation: Bulkheads isolate resources (e.g., separate thread pools per dependency) so a failure or overload in one area cannot exhaust resources and take down the whole system.

options.text: It isolates resources so one failing component cannot exhaust the whole system
It merges all thread pools into one for efficiency
It retries failed requests indefinitely
It caches all responses

questions.title: Kafka partition ordering
questions.description: In Apache Kafka, ordering of messages is guaranteed at what level?
questions.explanation: Kafka guarantees order only within a single partition, not across partitions of a topic; keys are used to route related messages to the same partition.

options.text: Within a single partition
Across the entire topic globally
Across all topics in the cluster
Only per consumer group with no partitions

questions.title: Choreography vs orchestration
questions.description: In microservice workflows, how does choreography differ from orchestration?
questions.explanation: In orchestration a central coordinator directs each step; in choreography services react to events independently with no central controller.

options.text: Choreography uses events with no central coordinator; orchestration uses a central controller
They are identical patterns
Choreography always uses a central controller
Orchestration forbids any messaging

questions.title: Outbox pattern
questions.description: What problem does the transactional outbox pattern solve?
questions.explanation: It avoids the dual-write problem by writing the business change and an outbox event in one local transaction; a relay then publishes the event reliably.

options.text: Reliably publishing events without the dual-write problem
Encrypting messages before sending
Sharding the database
Load balancing consumers

questions.title: Gossip protocol
questions.description: How does a gossip protocol disseminate information in a cluster?
questions.explanation: Nodes periodically exchange state with a few random peers; information spreads epidemically across the cluster, scaling without a central coordinator.

options.text: Each node randomly shares state with a few peers, spreading info epidemically
A central server broadcasts to every node directly
Nodes never communicate state to each other
Only the leader holds all information

questions.title: Fan-out on write vs read
questions.description: In a social feed, 'fan-out on write' means:
questions.explanation: Fan-out on write pushes a new post into each follower's feed at write time (fast reads, costly for celebrities); fan-out on read assembles the feed at query time.

options.text: Pushing a new post into each follower's feed when it is created
Assembling each user's feed only when they request it
Replicating the database to every region
Sharding the user table by ID

questions.title: REST statelessness
questions.description: What does it mean that REST is stateless?
questions.explanation: Each request must contain all information needed to process it; the server stores no client session context between requests.

options.text: Each request carries all needed context; the server keeps no client session state
The server remembers the client between requests
Responses are never cacheable
The client cannot send any data

questions.title: HTTP idempotent methods
questions.description: Which HTTP method is expected to be idempotent and safe (no side effects)?
questions.explanation: GET is both safe (no state change) and idempotent. PUT and DELETE are idempotent but not safe; POST is generally neither.

options.text: GET
POST
PATCH
CONNECT

questions.title: Rate limiting purpose
questions.description: Why do APIs apply rate limiting to clients?
questions.explanation: Rate limiting protects the service from abuse and overload and ensures fair resource sharing across clients.

options.text: To prevent abuse/overload and ensure fair usage
To encrypt request bodies
To guarantee responses are cached
To replace authentication

questions.title: Token bucket algorithm
questions.description: How does the token bucket rate-limiting algorithm allow bursts?
questions.explanation: Tokens accrue at a fixed rate up to a capacity; a request consumes a token, so accumulated tokens permit short bursts up to the bucket size.

options.text: Accumulated tokens permit short bursts up to the bucket capacity
It strictly allows one request per fixed interval with no bursts
It blocks all requests once the first token is used
It never refills tokens

questions.title: gRPC vs REST
questions.description: Which is an advantage of gRPC over typical JSON/REST for internal service-to-service calls?
questions.explanation: gRPC uses HTTP/2 and binary Protocol Buffers, giving lower latency, smaller payloads, streaming, and strongly-typed contracts.

options.text: Compact binary payloads, HTTP/2 streaming, and typed contracts
It is easier to call directly from a browser without tooling
It requires no schema definition
It only works over HTTP/1.0

questions.title: GraphQL over-fetching
questions.description: Which REST problem does GraphQL specifically aim to reduce?
questions.explanation: GraphQL lets clients request exactly the fields they need in one query, reducing over-fetching and under-fetching common with fixed REST endpoints.

options.text: Over-fetching and under-fetching of data
TLS certificate management
Database replication lag
DNS resolution time

questions.title: API pagination
questions.description: Why is cursor-based pagination often preferred over offset-based pagination for large, changing datasets?
questions.explanation: Offset pagination can skip or duplicate rows when data changes between pages and gets slow at high offsets; cursors anchor to a stable position.

options.text: It avoids skipped/duplicated rows and stays fast as the dataset grows
It always returns the entire dataset at once
It removes the need for any ordering
It encrypts the page contents

questions.title: DNS role
questions.description: What is the primary function of DNS?
questions.explanation: DNS resolves human-readable domain names into IP addresses so clients can connect to servers.

options.text: Translating domain names into IP addresses
Encrypting HTTP traffic
Balancing database load
Storing user sessions

questions.title: API gateway responsibilities
questions.description: Which responsibility is commonly centralized in an API gateway?
questions.explanation: Gateways centralize cross-cutting concerns like authentication, rate limiting, routing, and request aggregation in front of microservices.

options.text: Authentication, rate limiting, and routing for backend services
Running database migrations
Storing the primary copy of business data
Compiling backend source code

questions.title: Circuit breaker pattern
questions.description: What does a circuit breaker do when a downstream dependency keeps failing?
questions.explanation: After a failure threshold, the breaker 'opens' and fails fast (or returns a fallback) instead of waiting on the failing dependency, giving it time to recover.

options.text: It trips open and fails fast to avoid hammering the failing dependency
It retries the dependency as fast as possible forever
It restarts the database
It caches all successful responses permanently

questions.title: Exponential backoff
questions.description: Why should clients use exponential backoff with jitter when retrying failed requests?
questions.explanation: Increasing delays reduce load on a struggling server, and jitter prevents many clients from retrying in synchronized waves (the retry storm).

options.text: To reduce load on a recovering server and avoid synchronized retry storms
To retry immediately and repeatedly with no delay
To guarantee the request eventually succeeds
To encrypt the retry payload

questions.title: HTTP 429 meaning
questions.description: What does an HTTP 429 status code indicate?
questions.explanation: 429 Too Many Requests means the client has been rate limited and should slow down, often respecting a Retry-After header.

options.text: Too Many Requests — the client is being rate limited
The server crashed
The resource was not found
Authentication failed

questions.title: Microservices vs monolith trade-off
questions.description: Which is a genuine trade-off of microservices compared to a monolith?
questions.explanation: Microservices enable independent deployment and scaling but add operational complexity: networking, distributed debugging, and data consistency across services.

options.text: Independent scaling/deploys at the cost of operational and consistency complexity
They eliminate all network calls
They always reduce total infrastructure cost
They make distributed transactions trivial

questions.title: TLS termination location
questions.description: Where is TLS commonly terminated in a typical web architecture?
questions.explanation: TLS is often terminated at the load balancer or reverse proxy/CDN edge, with internal traffic on a trusted network (or re-encrypted for zero trust).

options.text: At the load balancer or reverse proxy / edge
Only inside the database
In the browser's local storage
It is never terminated anywhere

questions.title: Observability pillars
questions.description: Which three signals are commonly described as the pillars of observability?
questions.explanation: Logs, metrics, and traces together let operators understand system behavior and diagnose issues.

options.text: Logs, metrics, and traces
CPU, RAM, and disk
Users, sessions, and cookies
Encryption, hashing, and signing

questions.title: Distributed tracing
questions.description: What does a distributed trace let you do?
questions.explanation: A trace follows a single request across services via a propagated trace/span context, revealing where latency and errors occur in the call chain.

options.text: Follow one request across services to locate latency and errors
Measure only total CPU usage of the cluster
Encrypt traffic between services
Back up the database

questions.title: Blue-green deployment
questions.description: What is the main advantage of a blue-green deployment?
questions.explanation: Two identical environments let you switch traffic to the new version instantly and roll back quickly by switching back, minimizing downtime.

options.text: Near-instant cutover and easy rollback by switching environments
It requires only one environment
It removes the need for testing
It shards the database automatically

questions.title: Canary release
questions.description: What is a canary release?
questions.explanation: A canary routes a small percentage of traffic to the new version first to catch problems before a full rollout.

options.text: Rolling out a new version to a small subset of traffic first
Deploying to 100% of users instantly
Rolling back to the previous version
Disabling monitoring during deploys

questions.title: Idempotent infrastructure (IaC)
questions.description: Why is idempotency valued in infrastructure-as-code tools?
questions.explanation: Applying the same configuration repeatedly converges to the same desired state without unintended duplicate resources or drift.

options.text: Re-applying the same config yields the same end state without duplication
It guarantees zero-downtime deploys
It encrypts all secrets automatically
It removes the need for version control

questions.title: Authentication vs authorization
questions.description: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
questions.explanation: Authentication verifies who you are; authorization decides what you are allowed to do.

options.text: Authentication verifies identity; authorization grants permissions
They are the same thing
Authentication grants permissions; authorization verifies identity
Both only apply to databases

questions.title: JWT stateless auth
questions.description: What is one advantage of using signed JWTs for authentication?
questions.explanation: A signed JWT carries the claims and can be verified without a database/session lookup, enabling stateless auth across services.

options.text: Servers can verify the token without a central session store lookup
Tokens can be revoked instantly with no extra mechanism
The payload is automatically encrypted and hidden
They never expire

questions.title: JWT revocation challenge
questions.description: What is a known challenge with stateless JWTs?
questions.explanation: Because they are self-contained and valid until expiry, revoking a JWT before expiry requires extra state like a denylist, partly defeating statelessness.

options.text: Immediate revocation requires extra server-side state like a denylist
They cannot carry any user claims
They require a session lookup on every request
They cannot be signed

questions.title: Webhook reliability
questions.description: What should a webhook receiver do to handle the provider's retries safely?
questions.explanation: Providers retry on non-2xx or timeouts, so receivers must process webhooks idempotently (e.g., dedupe by event ID) to avoid double-processing.

options.text: Process events idempotently, deduplicating by event ID
Assume each event is delivered exactly once
Respond with 500 to acknowledge success
Ignore retries entirely

questions.title: Caching with ETags
questions.description: How does an ETag reduce bandwidth for repeated requests?
questions.explanation: The client sends the ETag via If-None-Match; if unchanged, the server returns 304 Not Modified with no body, saving transfer of the full resource.

options.text: The server can return 304 Not Modified with no body if the ETag matches
It compresses the response body
It encrypts the response
It forces the client to always re-download

questions.title: Zero-downtime schema migration
questions.description: Which approach supports a zero-downtime change when renaming a database column used by a live service?
questions.explanation: Use an expand/contract migration: add the new column, dual-write/backfill, switch reads, then drop the old column once nothing depends on it.

options.text: Expand/contract: add new column, backfill and dual-write, then drop the old
Rename the column in place during peak traffic
Take the database offline and rename it
Drop the column first, then recreate it

questions.title: Read-your-writes consistency
questions.description: Which technique helps provide read-your-writes consistency when reading from replicas?
questions.explanation: Route a user's reads to the primary (or to a replica known to have applied their write) for a short window after they write, so they see their own update.

options.text: Temporarily route that user's reads to the primary after a write
Disable replication entirely
Always read from the most lagging replica
Cache the write only on the client and never persist it

questions.title: Bloom filter use
questions.description: How does a Bloom filter help a storage engine avoid unnecessary disk reads?
questions.explanation: A Bloom filter quickly answers 'definitely not present' or 'possibly present' for a key, letting the engine skip disk lookups for keys it cannot contain (no false negatives).

options.text: It can rule out keys that are definitely absent, skipping disk lookups
It guarantees a key is present with no false positives
It stores the full value of each key
It sorts data on disk

questions.title: Multi-region active-active
questions.description: What is the hardest challenge in an active-active multi-region deployment that accepts writes in every region?
questions.explanation: Concurrent writes in different regions can conflict; resolving these conflicts (last-writer-wins, CRDTs, app logic) while keeping low latency is the core difficulty.

options.text: Resolving conflicting concurrent writes across regions
Translating domain names to IPs
Choosing a programming language
Compressing log files

questions.title: Sidecar pattern
questions.description: What is the sidecar pattern in a service mesh?
questions.explanation: A sidecar is a helper process/container deployed alongside a service to handle cross-cutting concerns (e.g., proxying, mTLS, telemetry) without changing app code.

options.text: A co-located proxy handling networking/observability for the main service
A backup copy of the database
A second region for failover
A queue for failed messages

questions.title: Hot vs cold storage tiering
questions.description: Why move infrequently accessed data to a 'cold' storage tier?
questions.explanation: Cold tiers are cheaper but slower; moving rarely accessed data there reduces cost while keeping hot data on fast, more expensive storage.

options.text: To cut cost by keeping rarely accessed data on cheaper, slower storage
To make that data faster to access
To enforce ACID transactions
To improve write consistency
